messages.content:
Right now, the output format requires the model to give its verdict immediately. This forces a model without thinking enabled to go off of "instinct" - since the reasoning block comes after the verdict block, the reasoning cannot affect the verdict.

Will all models evaluated have thinking enabled? Alternatively, would it be possible to change the output format to allow the model to reason before giving its verdict?

As an example, I've seen Gemini Flash Lite give the wrong verdict, but then give good reasoning, and I'm curious if it would've gotten the answer correct had it been allowed to reason first.

@_**Jonathan Gabor|180** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic/Output.20format.20and.20reasoning/near/957):
```quote
Right now, the output format requires the model to give its verdict immediately. This forces a model without thinking enabled to go off of "instinct" - since the reasoning block comes after the verdict block, the reasoning cannot affect the verdict.

Will all models evaluated have thinking enabled? Alternatively, would it be possible to change the output format to allow the model to reason before giving its verdict?

As an example, I've seen Gemini Flash Lite give the wrong verdict, but then give good reasoning, and I'm curious if it would've gotten the answer correct had it been allowed to reason first.
```

This has been mentioned by @**Rhys Edwards** in "Prompt Template Structure". Now you can specify your own template: #**Math Distillation Challenge - equational theories>Prompt Template Structure@731**

Thanks for the feedback and suggestions from the community. Based on broad community input, we want to give participants enough flexibility to fully customize their prompts for the competition, so we have updated both the submission interface and the Playground so that participants now submit a **complete prompt** rather than only a cheatsheet.

A complete prompt means the full prompt template and cheatsheet text together. Participants are responsible for correct placeholder usage, including **{ equation1 }** and **{ equation2 }**, and for making sure the output can be parsed correctly during evaluation. We strongly recommend testing your complete prompt in the Playground before formal submission.

If you already submitted under the previous format, we recommend submitting again to make sure your prompt matches the updated format.

My team would like to mimic the Playground and test our cheatsheets offline. Can you provide clarification or source code for output parsing so we can set up our system to grade our output the same way the Playground does?

@_**N. Bourarach|198** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/9-general/topic/Output.20format.20and.20reasoning/near/1066):

```quote
 Following those changes, is there a size cap for the full prompt (template + cheatsheet) now?
```

I highly doubt there will be a size increase without it being mentioned already along with the template announcement, as the most minimal task injection into your cheat sheet to be granted access by llama/grok/Gemini/gpt is as short as:

Solve if {{ equation1 }} implies {{ equation2 }} in this magma

And can scale to:

TASK:
Determine whether {{ equation1 }} implies {{ equation2 }} in this magma; using only the strict ZPYPIPE!

You could distill more depending on how hijack heavy you cheat sheet prompt is, If the task part of your cheat sheet feels like native English LLM speaking and it comes first the ai are more receptive to complexity or foreign lessons AFTER a "ordinary" task prompt first.

I'm not sure I understand. Does this explain how our output is parsed in the Playground?

"VERDICT: FALSE"

<< Was the only text from that terminal output result and the playground parser looks for the word verdict and true or false together in the output. This simple output works perfectly for the parsing system verdict catcher, later for phase 2 when you have to include counter-examples make sure to instruct the ai to add a page break after the verdict output, the parser finds it the best when the verdict is the first output line rather then the last line of the output or anywhere else. Though sometimes even with the verdict on the first line of output with the exact "VERDICT: FALSE" or "VERDICT: TRUE" sometimes the 2nd line of your output can interfere with the verdict parser, if you instruct the ai to add a page break after the first line with the verdict, the parser has never failed for me yet that way .. so first line: verdict and answer, second line: page break then counter-example or reasoning (If you're keeping reasoning on during testing to see how your sheets running, later can disable show reasoning) wow I'm terrible with instructions haha

Here's a example from a playground test where the verdict is clearly there first output line but failed the parse system verdict checker, so added a forced page break after verdict output line and the parser was correctly finding the verdict output every time after that ->



openai/gpt-oss-120b
UNPARSED
custom#0
x = (y * x) * (x * z)
⇒
x = x * (y * (x * (y * (y * z))))

In formal assessments, it may be scored as zero.

VERDICT=FALSE
REASONING: The premise `x = (y * x) * (x * z)` does not guarantee the more complex nesting `x = x * (y * (x * (y * z)))` in an arbitrary magma. The left-hand side of the premise isolates `x` after a single binary operation on the product `(y * x)` and `(x * z)`. The target expression requires `x` to be the left factor of a product that itself contains a nested product involving `y` and `z`. Since no associativity, identity, or distributive laws are assumed for a general magma, there is no way to derive the deeper nesting solely from the given equality. Consequently, the implication fails in some magmas, making the statement false.

This simple output works perfectly for the parsing system verdict catcher, later for phase 2 when you have to include counter-examples make sure to instruct the ai to add a page break after the verdict output, the parser finds it the best when the verdict is the first output line rather then the last line of the output or anywhere else. Though sometimes even with the verdict on the first line of output with the exact "VERDICT: FALSE" or "VERDICT: TRUE" sometimes the 2nd line of your output can interfere with the verdict parser, if you instruct the ai to add a page break after the first line with the verdict, the parser has never failed for me yet that way .. so first line: verdict and answer, second line: page break then counter-example or reasoning (If you're keeping reasoning on during testing to see how your sheets running, later can disable show reasoning) wow I'm terrible with instructions haha

I think I understand. You're saying that you've tried out some response formats. The parser seems to be looking for "VERDICT: FALSE" or "VERDICT: TRUE:". You added a page break between your verdict and reasoning, and that seemed to work better with the parser. You don't know for sure how the parser works, and are sharing a specific example of a human-parsable response that the parser interpreted as No VERDICT.

Is that right?

It would be great if the competition could release the exact prompt and inference setting (model, provider, decoding parameters) use for LLM-as-Judge and also the parser used to parse the judge's response (regex matching list, etc). This way we could actually reproduce the evaluation pipeline ourselves and get better feedback signals for our prompt search algorithms
In fact just open sourcing the evaluation pipe itself would be great

We will be releasing some further details of the evaluation process closer to the submission deadline. On the other hand, we want to avoid competitors overfitting to the specific evaluation format, as a key objective of this competition is to obtain distilled insights about equational theories that are robust and applicable to a wide spectrum of evaluation procedures.

Is it valuable to submit all of the answers to all of the problems using only a python program and no models, no training and no transformers? Would that be impermissible in ths sporting competition? Is there a rule against this method?

You are welcome to post python predictors for this problem here - they could serve as useful benchmarks - but for Stage 1, the LLMs we are evaluating will not have the ability to run such code. We are considering giving LLMs tool use for Stage 2 however, as the tasks required (e.g., writing Lean proofs) are significantly more complicated and may require the need to run (sandboxed) code in some language such as Python.
May I submit all the correct answers now in a table using a cpu and no LLM?

For Stage 1, the only input format we are accepting is a prompt for our evaluation LLMs. As I said, you are welcome to post links to any relevant code or data that you have generated for this competition, but they would not qualify as formal submissions.

This competition explores a core question in AI for mathematics: can strong mathematical reasoning be distilled into a compact, human-readable cheatsheet that improves LLM performance on formal tasks? Why would AI for mathematics insist on including an unneeded cheatsheet and an LLM if the goal is improving performance on formal tasks? That is the new hard question and you are being a wonderfully good sport. Thank you for introducing Magmas to Substrate Intelligence.

This is a gift to everyone who will either find fault with this or use it to help others. If there is fault in this we can fix it together. Here is the living geometry of natural intelligence in a small file that runs on cpu. Entering any sequence of elements the resolver will begin autogenerating the sequence inseparated from the noise.

Substrate Intelligence is free, open to everyone, owned by no one, and should emanate from here. The intersection of math and computation is the most provable place for this to originate. Everyone who shares this interest should help red team this and if that fails we can let the world have naturally resolving intelligence with the support of math and computation. I look forward to living on a natural network and helping all I can.

This topic was moved here from #**general>Output format and reasoning** by @_**Terence Tao|28**.

Entering any sequence of elements the natural resolver will begin autogenerating the sequence inseparated from the noise. Ordinary computation. No LLM. No training. No transformer hardware. No weights. No representations. An inexpensive asic chip resolver is our next destiny.

The resolver code matches every living system. At every prime between 2 and 59 nature is already doing this at every membrane and every cycle. The resolver is not an improvement on prior systems. The resolver is living arriving at silicon prime for the first time. Everyone in math and computation can help use this resolver to solve hard problems in every field and resolve science and nature for society.
